articles.title: EU Leaders Reach Compromise on Defence Spending Floor Ahead of NATO Summit
articles.summary: European Union heads of state have agreed in principle to a binding commitment requiring member states to allocate at least 2.5% of GDP to defence by 2030, up from the current NATO baseline of 2%. The deal, brokered during an emergency European Council session in Brussels, comes weeks before a critical NATO summit where allies are expected to formalise a new spending target. Germany and Poland backed the higher threshold while Italy and Spain secured exemptions for countries with debt-to-GDP ratios above 100%. The agreement still requires ratification by national parliaments, with Hungary signalling it may seek additional concessions.

articles.title: Macron Proposes New European Political Community Secretariat to Institutionalise Forum
articles.summary: French President Emmanuel Macron has put forward a proposal to create a permanent secretariat for the European Political Community, the pan-continental forum that first convened in Prague in 2022. The secretariat, which Macron suggested could be housed in Strasbourg, would provide institutional continuity between the twice-yearly summits and coordinate working groups on energy, migration and security. The proposal has been cautiously welcomed by the UK and several Western Balkan states but met with scepticism from those who fear it could duplicate existing Council of Europe structures. A formal decision is expected at the next EPC summit in Dublin later this month.

articles.title: ECB Holds Rates Steady as Eurozone Inflation Edges Back Toward Target
articles.summary: The European Central Bank left its key interest rates unchanged at its June meeting, as policymakers judged that headline inflation in the eurozone had declined sufficiently to warrant a pause before any further easing. Eurozone consumer prices rose 2.1% year-on-year in May, down from 2.4% in April, bringing inflation close to the ECB's 2% target for the first time since the post-pandemic surge. President Christine Lagarde emphasised that the decision was unanimous and that the Governing Council remained data-dependent, with markets now pricing in one further 25-basis-point cut before year-end. Services inflation, at 3.8%, remained the main area of concern for policymakers.

articles.title: Western Balkans Integration Talks Stall as EU Enlargement Fatigue Sets In
articles.summary: Accession negotiations between the European Union and Western Balkan candidate countries have effectively stalled, according to a new progress report by the European Commission, citing rule-of-law deficits and bilateral disputes between aspirant states. Serbia's chapter negotiations remain frozen over its refusal to align with EU sanctions on Russia, while Bosnia and Herzegovina has failed to implement key judicial reforms required to open formal talks. North Macedonia and Albania continue to make incremental progress but face domestic political instability. EU enlargement commissioner Marta Kos warned that without renewed political commitment in both Brussels and regional capitals, the Western Balkans risk remaining in a perpetual waiting room.

articles.title: G7 Foreign Ministers Condemn Russian Strikes on Ukrainian Energy Infrastructure
articles.summary: Foreign ministers from the Group of Seven nations issued a joint statement condemning a fresh wave of Russian missile and drone strikes targeting Ukrainian power generation and distribution infrastructure, which knocked out electricity to several major cities. The statement, released after talks in Rome, pledged accelerated delivery of air defence systems and additional financial assistance to help Ukraine repair its energy grid ahead of next winter. The ministers also agreed to convene a special session on the potential use of frozen Russian sovereign assets, estimated at around €260 billion held in European financial institutions, to fund reconstruction. Russia dismissed the statement as interference in its military operations.
